bibliographic_data.title: The Magellan-TESS Survey I: Survey Description and Mid-Survey Results
bibliographic_data.abstract: Kepler revealed that roughly one-third of Sun-like stars host planets orbiting within 100 days and between the size of Earth and Neptune. How do these planets form, what are they made of, and do they represent a continuous population or multiple populations? To help address these questions, we began the Magellan-TESS Survey (MTS), which uses Magellan II/PFS to obtain radial velocity (RV) masses of 30 TESS-detected exoplanets and develops an analysis framework that connects observed planet distributions to underlying populations. In the past, small planet RV measurements have been challenging to obtain due to host star faintness and low RV semi-amplitudes, and challenging to interpret due to the potential biases in target selection and observation planning decisions. The MTS attempts to minimize these biases by focusing on bright TESS targets and employing a quantitative selection function and observing strategy. In this paper, we (1) describe our motivation and survey strategy, (2) present our first catalog of planet density constraints for 27 TESS Objects of Interest (TOIs; 22 in our population analysis sample, 12 that are members of the same systems), and (3) employ a hierarchical Bayesian model to produce preliminary constraints on the mass-radius (M-R) relation. We find that the biases causing previous M-R relations to predict fairly high masses at 1 R⊕ have been reduced. This work can inform more detailed studies of

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces the Magellan-TESS Survey (MTS) to obtain radial velocity masses for TESS-detected exoplanets and develops a new analysis framework.
core_content.key_methodology: The MTS uses Magellan II/PFS to measure RV of 30 TESS-detected exoplanets, focusing on bright targets with a quantitative selection function and observing strategy to minimize biases in target selection and observation planning decisions.
outcomes: Catalog of planet density constraints for 27 TESS Objects of Interest (TOIs)
Preliminary constraints on the mass-radius relation
core_content.full_text_markdown: The Magellan-TESS Survey I: Survey Description and Mid-Survey Results aims to address questions about how planets form, what they are made of, and whether they represent a continuous population or multiple populations. To achieve this, the study introduces the Magellan-TESS Survey (MTS), which uses the Magellan II/PFS telescope to obtain radial velocity (RV) masses for 30 TESS-detected exoplanets. The survey focuses on bright targets with a quantitative selection function and observing strategy to minimize biases in target selection and observation planning decisions. This paper describes the motivation and survey strategy of MTS, presents the first catalog of planet density constraints for 27 TESS Objects of Interest (TOIs), and employs a hierarchical Bayesian model to produce preliminary constraints on the mass-radius (M-R) relation. The results show that biases in previous M-R relations have been reduced.
